items.q: Is a SWOT analysis a strategy in itself?
items.correct: No — SWOT organises internal and external factors; strategy is the chosen plan of action that follows, matching strengths to opportunities and addressing weaknesses and threats.
items.misconception: Completing the SWOT grid means the strategic work is done.
items.random_wrong: SWOT is the name of a department in large firms.
items.why: Students submit analysis tools as answers to 'recommend a strategy' questions.

items.q: Do Porter's five forces describe a company's internal strengths?
items.correct: No — the five forces analyse the attractiveness of an industry: rivalry, threat of entry, substitutes, and buyer and supplier power; they are external, industry-level factors.
items.misconception: Five forces analysis tells you how good the company itself is.
items.random_wrong: The five forces are the firm's five biggest customers.
items.why: Mixing firm-level and industry-level analysis muddles strategy answers.

items.q: Is intuition worthless in business decision-making?
items.correct: No — data-driven approaches reduce bias, but experienced judgement matters when data is scarce, time is short or the situation is novel; many decisions blend both.
items.misconception: Only decisions backed by quantitative data are valid.
items.random_wrong: Intuition is banned in publicly listed companies.
items.why: IB expects a balanced view of scientific versus intuitive decision-making.
Correct answer: Threat of entry. “Buyer power” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Who created the five forces framework?
items.why: It's Porter. “Ansoff” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Porter described how many competitive forces?
items.why: The real figure is 5. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
